title: Lagoon Congee with Saffron Brodetto & Sardine Floss
tagline: Venetian rice porridge from the century the Song fleet ruled the Adriatic.
scenario: 1204. Pressed relentlessly south by the Jin, the Song court makes a decision our timeline never saw: rather than retreat to Hangzhou, it sends its treasure fleet — the largest ships on Earth — west along the monsoon routes its merchants already know, seeking a defensible harbor beyond any steppe army's reach. In 1203 the fleet winters in Alexandria; in 1204, while Venice's own galleys are away sacking Constantinople, three hundred Song vessels glide into an undefended lagoon. The occupation is strangely bloodless — the Song want a port, not a massacre — and within a decade Venice is the westernmost prefecture of a maritime Song state. Rice paddies replace salt pans on the terraferma. In the campo kitchens, Chinese congee technique meets what the lagoon actually offers: sardines, saffron brought up the old spice routes, wine must, no soy for a thousand miles. Fish is preserved the Song way — cooked, shredded, dried in a pan to floss — but seasoned with garum's descendant, colatura. This bowl is what a Rialto breakfast becomes.
scenarioSummary: 1204 — The exiled Song treasure fleet seizes an undefended Venice, and congee technique collides with lagoon sardines and spice-route saffron.
decoys: 1348 — The Black Death spares Sicily alone; its fishermen invent a fortifying rice porridge served in the plague wards of the mainland.
1897 — A catastrophic anchovy failure forces Ligurian monasteries to stretch risotto into porridge, thickened with dried fish shreds.

items.item: vialone nano or carnaroli rice, rinsed
light fish broth (or 1.5 L water + 500 ml clam juice)
fresh sardine fillets (from ~12 sardines), pin bones in — they dissolve
olive oil, plus more to finish
saba or vincotto (grape must syrup), plus 1 tbsp red wine vinegar

method: Quick-pickle the onion: warm the saba with the vinegar and a pinch of salt, pour over the sliced red onion, and set aside — it will turn glossy and dark, somewhere between agrodolce and Chinese sweet pickle.

Start the congee: combine rice, broth, bay leaf, lemon peel and salt in a heavy pot. Boil, then drop to the barest simmer, lid ajar, stirring every 10 minutes. Cook 50–60 minutes until the grains have burst into a loose, silky porridge. Add hot water if it tightens; it should pour slowly off a ladle.
Make the floss while the congee cooks: steam or gently poach the sardine fillets for 4 minutes until just done. Drain very well and break up with a fork.

Heat the olive oil in a wide nonstick pan over medium-low. Add the sardines, colatura, honey and fennel seed. Now the patient part: stir and press with the fork continuously, 12–15 minutes, as the fish dries from paste to shreds to a light, fluffy, deeply savory floss. It's done when it's dry to the touch and pulls apart like coarse wool. Tip onto paper towel.

Bloom the saffron in 2 tbsp hot water for 10 minutes. In a small pan, soften the shallot in olive oil, add the wine, and reduce by two-thirds. Add the saffron and its water, a pinch of salt, and simmer 2 minutes to a loose golden glaze — this is the brodetto.

Ladle the congee into warm bowls. Spoon a ring of saffron brodetto over each, pile a nest of sardine floss in the center, and finish with drained pickled onion, black pepper and a thread of olive oil.
Eat immediately, stirring the saffron in as you go. There is no soy sauce on this table — colatura got there first by about eleven centuries.

verification.verdict: No published recipe combines rice congee with a saffron reduction and sardine floss. Nearest neighbors: sardine congees (whole fillets, East Asian seasonings) and fish-floss congees (no saffron, no Venetian pantry).
